html:
XI

That Which Remains

I

Through September Neville had nursed Gerda by day and worked by night. The middle of October, just when they usually moved into town for the winter, she collapsed, had what the doctor called a nervous breakdown.

тАЬYouтАЩve been overworking,тАЭ he told her. тАЬYouтАЩre not strong enough in these days to stand hard brain-work. You must give it up.тАЭ

For a fortnight she lay tired and passive, surrendered and inert, caring for nothing but to give up and lie still and drink hot milk. Then she struggled up and mooned about the house and garden, and cried weakly from time to time, and felt depressed and bored, and as if life were over and she were at the bottom of the sea.

тАЬThis must be what mother feels,тАЭ she thought. тАЬPoor mother.тБатАКтБатАж IтАЩm like her; IтАЩve had my life, and IтАЩm too stupid to work, and I can only cry.тБатАКтБатАж Men must work and women must weep.тБатАКтБатАж I never knew before that that was true.тБатАКтБатАж I mustnтАЩt see mother just now, it would be the last strawтБатАКтБатАж like the skeletons people used to look at to warn themselves what they would come to.тБатАКтБатАж Poor motherтБатАКтБатАж and poor me.тБатАКтБатАж But motherтАЩs getting better now sheтАЩs being analysed. That wouldnтАЩt help me at all. I analyse myself too much already.тБатАКтБатАж And I was so happy a few months ago. What a dreadful end to a good ambition. I shall never work again, I suppose, in any way that counts. So thatтАЩs that.тБатАКтБатАж Why do I want to work and to do something? Other wives and mothers donтАЩt.тБатАКтБатАж Or do they, only they donтАЩt know it, because they donтАЩt analyse? I believe they do, lots of them. Or is it only my horrible egotism and vanity, that canтАЩt take a back seat quietly? I was always like that, I know. Nan and I and Gilbert. Not Jim so much, and not Pamela at all. But RodneyтАЩs worse than I am; he wouldnтАЩt want to be counted out, put on the shelf, in the forties; heтАЩd be frightfully sick if he had to stand by and see other people working and getting on and in the thick of things when he wasnтАЩt. He couldnтАЩt bear it; heтАЩd take to drink, I think.тБатАКтБатАж I hope Rodney wonтАЩt ever have a nervous breakdown and feel like this, poor darling, heтАЩd be dreadfully tiresome.тБатАКтБатАж Not to work after all. Not to be a doctor.тБатАКтБатАж What then? Just go about among people, grinning like a dog. Winter in town, talking, dining, being the political wife. Summer in the country, walking, riding, reading, playing tennis. Fun, of course. But whatтАЩs it all for? When IтАЩve got Gerda off my hands I shall have done being a mother, in any sense that matters. Is being a wife enough to live for? RodneyтАЩs wife? Oh, I want to be some use, want to do things, to count.тБатАКтБатАж And Rodney will die some timeтБатАФI know heтАЩll die firstтБатАФand then I shanтАЩt even be a wife. And in twenty years I shanтАЩt be able to do things with my body much more, and what then? What will be left?тБатАКтБатАж I think IтАЩm getting hysterical, like poor mother.тБатАКтБатАж How ugly I look, these days.тАЭ

She stopped before the looking-glass. Her face looked back at her, white and thin, almost haggard, traced in the last few weeks for the first time with definite lines round brow and mouth. Her dark hair was newly streaked with grey.

тАЬMiddle age,тАЭ said Neville, and a cold hand was laid round her heart. тАЬIt had to come some time, and this illness has opened the door to it. Or shall I look young again when IтАЩm quite well? No, never young again.тАЭ

She shivered.

тАЬI look like mother today.тБатАКтБатАж I am like mother.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

So youth and beauty were to leave her, too. She would recover from this illness and this extinguishing of charm, but not completely, and not for long. Middle age had begun. She would have off days in future, when she would look old and worn instead of always, as hitherto, looking charming. She wouldnтАЩt, in future, be sure of herself; people wouldnтАЩt be sure to think тАЬA lovely woman, Mrs.┬аRodney Bendish.тАЭ Soon they would be saying тАЬHow old Mrs.┬аBendish is getting to look,тАЭ and then тАЬShe was a pretty woman once.тАЭ

Well, looks didnтАЩt matter much really, after all.тБатАКтБатАж

тАЬThey do, they do,тАЭ cried Neville to the glass, passionately truthful. тАЬIf youтАЩre vain they doтБатАФand I am vain. Vain of my mind and of my body.тБатАКтБатАж Vanity, vanity, all is vanityтБатАКтБатАж and now the silver cord is going to be loosed and the golden bowl is going to be broken, and I shall be hurt.тАЭ

Looks did matter. It was no use canting, and minimising them. They affected the thing that mattered mostтБатАФoneтАЩs relations with people. Men, for instance, cared more to talk to a woman whose looks pleased them. They liked pretty girls, and pretty women. Interesting men cared to talk to them: they told them things they would never tell a plain woman. Rodney did. He liked attractive women. Sometimes he made love to them, prettily and harmlessly.

The thought of Rodney stabbed her. If Rodney were to get to care lessтБатАКтБатАж to stop making love to herтБатАКтБатАж worse, to stop needing her.тБатАКтБатАж For he did need her; through all their relationship, disappointing in some of its aspects, his need had persisted, a simple, demanding thing.

Humour suddenly came back.

тАЬThis, I suppose, is what Gerda is anticipating, and why she wonтАЩt have Barry tied to her. If Rodney wasnтАЩt tied to me he could flee from my wrinkles.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬOh, what an absurd fuss one makes. What does any of it matter? ItтАЩs all in the course of nature, and the sooner тАЩtis over the sooner to sleep. Middle age will be very nice and comfortable and entertaining, once oneтАЩs fairly in it.тБатАКтБатАж I go babbling about my wasted brain and fading looks as if IтАЩd been a mixture of Sappho and Helen of Troy.тБатАКтБатАж ThatтАЩs the worst of being a vain creature.тБатАКтБатАж What will Rosalind do when her time comes? Oh, paint, of course, and dyeтБатАФmore thickly than she does now, I mean. SheтАЩll be a ghastly sight. A raddled harridan. At least I shall always look respectable, I hope. I shall go down to Gerda. I want to look at something young. The young have their troubles, poor darlings, but they donтАЩt know how lucky they are.тАЭ

II

In November Neville and Gerda, now both convalescent, joined Rodney in their town flat. Rodney thought London would buck Neville up. London does buck you up, even if it is November and there is no gulf stream and not much coal. For there is always music and always people. Neville had a critical appreciation of both. Then, for comic relief, there are politics. You cannot be really bored with a world which contains the mother of Parliaments, particularly if her news is communicated to you at first hand by one of her members. Disgusted you may be and are, if you are a right-minded person, but at least not bored.

What variety, what excitement, what a moving picture show, is this tragic and comic planet! Why want to be useful, why indulge such tedious inanities as ambitions, why dream wistfully of doing oneтАЩs bit, making oneтАЩs work, in a world already as full of bits, bright, coloured, absurd bits, like a kaleidoscope, as full of marks (mostly black marks) as a novel from a free library? A dark and bad and bitter world, of course, full of folly, wickedness and misery, sick with poverty and pain, so that at times the only thing Neville could bear to do in it was to sit on some dreadful committee thinking of ameliorations for the lot of the very poor, or to go and visit Pamela in Hoxton and help her with some job or otherтБатАФthat kind of direct, immediate, human thing, which was a sop to uneasiness and pity such as the political work she dabbled in, however similar its ultimate aim, could never be.

I

To Pamela Neville said, тАЬAre you afraid of getting old, Pamela?тАЭ

Pamela replied, тАЬNot a bit. Are you?тАЭ And she confessed it.

тАЬOften itтАЩs like a cold douche of water down my spine, the thought of it. I reason and mock at myself, but I donтАЩt like it.тБатАКтБатАж YouтАЩre different; finer, more real, more unselfish. Besides, youтАЩll have done something worth doing when you have to give up. I shanтАЩt.тАЭ

PamelaтАЩs brows went up.

тАЬKay? Gerda? The pretty dears: IтАЩve done nothing so nice as them. YouтАЩve done whatтАЩs called a womanтАЩs work in the worldтБатАФisnтАЩt that the phrase?тАЭ

тАЬDone itтБатАФjust so, but so long ago. What now? I still feel young, Pamela, even now that I know IтАЩm not.тБатАКтБатАж Oh Lord, itтАЩs a queer thing, being a woman. A well-off woman of forty-three with everything made comfortable for her and her brain gone to pot and her work in the world done. I want something to bite my teeth intoтБатАФsome solid, permanent jobтБатАФand I get nothing but sweetmeats, and people point at Kay and Gerda and say тАШThatтАЩs your work, and itтАЩs over. Now you can rest, seeing that itтАЩs good, like God on the seventh day.тАЩтАКтАЭ

тАЬI donтАЩt say тАШNow you can rest. Except just now, while youтАЩre run down.тАЩтАКтАЭ

тАЬRun down, yes; run down like a disordered clock because I tried to tackle an honest job of work again. IsnтАЩt it sickening, Pamela? IsnтАЩt it ludicrous?тАЭ

тАЬLudicrousтБатАФno. Everyone comes up against his own limitations. YouтАЩve got to work within them thatтАЩs all. After all, there are plenty of jobs you can do that want doingтБатАФsimply shouting to be done.тАЭ

тАЬPammie dear, itтАЩs worse than IтАЩve said. IтАЩm a low creature. I donтАЩt only want to do jobs that want doing: I want to count, to make a name. IтАЩm damnably ambitious. YouтАЩll despise that, of courseтБатАФand youтАЩre quite right, it is despicable. But there it is. Most men and many women are tormented by itтБатАФthey itch for recognition.тАЭ

тАЬOf course. One is.тАЭ

тАЬYou too, Pammie?тАЭ

тАЬI have been. Less now. Life gets to look short, when youтАЩre thirty-nine.тАЭ

тАЬAh, but you have itтБатАФrecognition, even fame, in the world you work in. You count for something. If you value it, there it is. I wouldnтАЩt grumble if IтАЩd played your part in the piece. ItтАЩs a good partтБатАФa useful part and a speaking part.тАЭ

тАЬI suppose we all feel we should rather like to play someone elseтАЩs part for a change. ThereтАЩs nothing exciting about mine. Most people would far prefer yours.тАЭ

They would, of course; Neville knew it. The happy political wife rather than the unmarried woman worker; Rodney, Gerda and Kay for company rather than Frances Carr. There was no question which was the happier lot, the fuller, the richer, the easier, the more entertaining.

тАЬAh well.тБатАКтБатАж You see, Rosalind spent the afternoon with me yesterday, and I felt suddenly that it wasnтАЩt for me to be stuck up about herтБатАФwhat am I too but the pampered female idler, taking good things without earning them? It made me shudder. Hence this fit of blues. The pampered, lazy, brainless animalтБатАФit is such a terrific sight when in human form. Rosalind talked about Nan, Pamela. In her horrible wayтБатАФyou know. Hinting that she isnтАЩt alone in Rome, but with Stephen Lumley.тАЭ

Pamela took off her glasses and polished them.

тАЬRosalind would, of course. What did you say?тАЭ

тАЬI lost my temper. I let out at her. ItтАЩs not a thing I often do with RosalindтБатАФit doesnтАЩt seem worth while. But this time I saw red. I told her what I thought of her eternal gossip and scandal. I said, what if Nan and Stephen Lumley, or Nan and anyone else, did arrange to be in Rome at the same time and to see a lot of each other; where was the harm? No use. You canтАЩt pin Rosalind down. She just shrugged her shoulders and smiled, and said тАШMy dear, we all know our Nan. We all know too that Stephen Lumley has been in love with her for a year, and doesnтАЩt live with his wife. Then they go off to Rome at the same moment, and one hears that they are seen everywhere together. Why shut oneтАЩs eyes to obvious deductions? YouтАЩre so like an ostrich, Neville.тАЩ I said IтАЩd rather be an ostrich than a ferret, eternally digging into other peopleтАЩs concernsтБатАФand by the time we had got to that I thought it was far enough, so I had an engagement with my dressmaker.тАЭ

тАЬItтАЩs no use tackling Rosalind,тАЭ Pamela agreed. тАЬSheтАЩll never change her spots.тБатАКтБатАж Do you suppose itтАЩs true about Nan?тАЭ

тАЬI daresay it is. Yes, IтАЩm afraid I do think itтАЩs quite likely true.тБатАКтБатАж Nan was so queer the few times I saw her after GerdaтАЩs accident. I was unhappy about her. She was so hard, and so more than usually cynical and unget-at-able. She told me it had been all her fault, leading Gerda into mischief, doing circus tricks that the child tried to emulate and couldnтАЩt. I couldnтАЩt read her, quite. Her tone about Gerda had a queer edge to it. And she rather elaborately arranged, I thought, so that she shouldnтАЩt meet Barry. Pamela, do you think she had finally and absolutely turned Barry down before he took up so suddenly with Gerda, or.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

Pamela said, тАЬI know nothing. She told me nothing. But I rather thought, when she came to see me just before she went down to Cornwall, that she had made up her mind to have him. I may have been wrong.тАЭ

Neville leant her forehead on her hands and sighed.

тАЬOr you may have been right. And if you were right, itтАЩs the ghastliest tragedyтБатАФfor her.тБатАКтБатАж Oh, I shouldnтАЩt have let Gerda go and work with him; I should have known better.тБатАКтБатАж Nan had rebuffed him, and he flew off at a tangent, and there was Gerda sitting in his office, as pretty as flowers and with her funny little silent charm.тБатАКтБатАж And if Nan was all the time waiting for him, meaning to say yes when he asked her.тБатАКтБатАж Poor darling Nan, robbed by my horrid little girl, who doesnтАЩt even want to marry.тБатАКтБатАж If thatтАЩs the truth, it would account for the Stephen Lumley business. Nan wouldnтАЩt stay on in London, to see them together. If Lumley caught her at that psychological moment, sheтАЩd very likely go off with him, out of mere desperation and bravado. That would be so terribly like Nan.тБатАКтБатАж What a desperate, wry, cursed business life is.тБатАКтБатАж On the other hand, she may just be going about with Lumley on her own terms not his. ItтАЩs her own affair whichever way it is; what weтАЩve got to do is to contradict the stories Rosalind is spreading whenever we get the chance. Not that one can scotch scandal once it startsтБатАФparticularly RosalindтАЩs scandal.тАЭ

тАЬIgnore it. Nan can ignore it when she comes back. It wonтАЩt hurt her. NanтАЩs had plenty of things said about her before, true and untrue, and never cared.тАЭ

тАЬYouтАЩre splendid at the ignoring touch, Pam. I believe thereтАЩs nothing you canтАЩt and donтАЩt ignore.тАЭ

тАЬWell, why not? IgnoringтАЩs easy.тАЭ

тАЬNot for most of us. I believe it is, for you. In a sense you ignore life itself; anyhow you donтАЩt let it hold and bully you. When your time comes youтАЩll ignore age, and later death.тАЭ

тАЬThey donтАЩt matter much, do they? Does anything? I suppose itтАЩs my stolid temperament, but I canтАЩt feel that it does.тАЭ

Neville thought, as she had often thought before, that Pamela, like Nan, only more calmly, less recklessly and disdainfully, had the aristocratic touch. Pamela, with her delicate detachments and her light, even touch on things great and small, made her feel fussy and petty and excitable.

тАЬI suppose youтАЩre right, my dear.тБатАКтБатАж тАШAll is laughter, all is dust, all is nothingness, for the things that are arise out of the unreasonable.тБатАКтБатАжтАЩ I must get back. Give my love to FrancesтБатАКтБатАж and when next you see Gerda do try to persuade her that marriage is one of the things that donтАЩt matter and that she might just as well put up with to please us all. The child is a little nuisanceтБатАФas obstinate as a mule.тАЭ

IV

Neville, walking away from PamelaтАЩs grimy street in the November fog, felt that London was terrible. An ugly clamour of strident noises and hard, shrill voices, jabbering of vulgar, trivial things. A wry, desperate, cursed world, as she had called it, a pot seething with bitterness and all dreadfulness, with its Rosalinds floating on the top like scum.

And Nan, her Nan, her little vehement sister, whom she had mothered of old, had pulled out of countless scrapesтБатАФNan had now taken her life into her reckless hands and done what with it? Given it, perhaps, to a man she didnтАЩt love, throwing cynical defiance thereby at love, which had hurt her; escaping from the intolerable to the shoddy. Even if not, even supposing the best, Nan was hurt and in trouble; Neville was somehow sure of that. Men were blind fools; men were fickle children. Neville almost wished now that Barry would give up Gerda and go out to Rome and fetch Nan back. But, to do that, Barry would have to fall out of love with Gerda and into love again with Nan; and even Barry, Neville imagined, was not such a weathercock as that. And Barry would really be happier with Gerda. With all their differences, they were both earnest citizens, both keen on social progress. Nan was a cynical flibberty-gibbet; it might not have been a happy union. Perhaps happy unions were not for such as Nan. But at the thought of Nan playing that desperate game with Stephen Lumley in Rome, NevilleтАЩs face twitched.тБатАКтБатАж

She would go to Rome. She would see Nan; find out how things were. Nan always liked to see her, would put up with her even when she wanted no one else.

That was, at least, a job one could do. These family jobsтБатАФthey still go on, they never cease, even when one is getting middle-aged and oneтАЩs brain has gone to pot. They remain, always, the jobs of the affections.

She would write to Nan tonight, and tell her she was starting for Rome in a few days, to have a respite from the London fogs.

V

But she did not start for Rome, or even write to Nan, for when she got home she went to bed with influenza.
